item.title: Arch Manning isn’t backing down from expectations, and neither is Steve Sarkisian, as CFP semis aren't enough
item.description: Arch Manning addressed the 'bust' narrative at SEC Media Days, saying the criticism lights a fire as Texas eyes the college football playoff.
item.content:encoded:
TAMPA, FL - Taking the podium to an emptying out ballroom on Thursday afternoon at SEC Media Days, Steve Sarkisian did not shy away from the CFP expectations surrounding his Texas Longhorns this season.

After an early portion of the summer was seemingly dedicated to ruffling feathers within his own state of Texas, along with touching on the intricate courses of 'basketweaving' at Ole Miss, the attention finally turned to the lofty expectations that await the Longhorns this season.

Lane Kiffin goes off script: Hulk Hogan, Ole Miss and social media dominate SEC Media Days

This time last year, the conversation centered around the unrealistic preseason hype around his quarterback

"Arch Manning a year ago wasn’t on social media or television saying he was going to win the Heisman Trophy, or be the first pick in the draft," Steve Sarkisian said about the hype last year around Arch. "What he got put through would’ve melted 99% of the people in his shoes..

"What he got put through would’ve ruined 95% of the people in his shoes."

Some decided to put him in the Heisman Trophy conversation before he had actually taken meaningful snaps for the Longhorns, while others (hey there) kept the expectations at a reasonable position.

Even Arch was the one who had to remind reporters last year in Atlanta that he had not accomplished anything at Texas worthy enough to have him on the constant preseason awards watch list.

But now, after a second half progression last season for the Longhorns, Manning is worthy enough to be labeled a Heisman Trophy contender. It also helps that Steve Sarkisian loaded up in the transfer portal with talent to surround his quarterback on the offensive side of the ball, led by wide receiver Cam Coleman.

"Manning is a lot more confident," Sarkisian said about Arch. "I think last season, he found out a lot about himself. Any time we're face with adversity, and we got on the other side of it, you gain confidence. I think that you see a different leader in him, in the way he interacts with his teammates.

"He's very comfortable with who he is. He's not trying to be something that he's not."

Florida coach brings laughs, no filter and Lane Kiffin comparison rebuttal to first SEC media days

If there was ever a time to put Sarkisian on a list of coaches who needed to live up to the expectations, this would be the perfect opportunity.

"The expectations are a lot higher in our building than they are on the outiside," Steve Sarkisian told reporters.

Sure about that, Sark? Because most folks are expecting at the very least a CFP semifinal appearance this season.

Compared to last year's meeting with the media in Atlanta, this was obviously a different quarterback, with what seems like an urge to prove the doubters from last season wrong.

"No, there's nothing you can do. Everybody has an opinion and gets paid to talk in the media," Manning mentioned when asked about the 'bust' narrative last season. "I'm trying to control the controllables. But, it probably lights a little fire in me, and continue to try and prove myself one week at a time."

Pete Golding drops unfiltered truth on 'Ex' Lane Kiffin, and NCAA tampering: 'What have we created?'

Knowing his background, Arch will do everything in his power to prove some of those who doubted him wrong. But, he's not doing it for anyone outside of that Texas locker room.

What we saw on Thursday was a young man who has grown over the past two years. Even if that did not meet the timeline for some, Arch has the character and purpose to make sure he is at least giving the Longhorns everything possible to compete for a national championship this season.

Last year, Manning looked like a quarterback who might have the best media-prep in college football, but understood he was going to need time to develop into the leader, and player, Steve Sarkisian needed him to be in Austin.

Joking about his golf game, finally breaking 80 last week, mentioning that he has taken the time to just enjoy life during the off-season, this is a player who is not getting bent out of shape over the things he cannot control.

"The best Manning golfer? It's probably Peyton, yea," Arch responded with a smile about family golf outings.

He's most certainly grown up over the past year, that's obvious. But, acknowledging his mistakes and outcomes from the previous season is another sign of his maturity.

"Yea, I had a few games like that last year," Arch said about the Florida game last season. "

"We do a good job about not talking about NIL," Arch mentioned. "I don't know how it is at other schools, that's big for us. It's about the players, and not worrying about how much someone makes."

Right now, Steve Sarkisian can talk about the amount of tough games that await in the 2026 season, but the expectations inside and outside the program are for this Texas team to compete for a championship.

Now, sometimes the conference champion is usually a good indicator of national championship hopes. For Steve Sarkisian, getting past Georgia is a stepping-stone to taking the next step as a program.

I'm not implying one has to win the SEC, or Big Ten and any other conference, to win the CFP.

But, Sarkisian understands that reaching the mountain top means that you probably need to take care of the reigning champion beforehand.

"If you want to be the champion, you've got to beat the champion. We've lost to Georgia 3 times. Credit to Kirby, they've done a great job. If we want to be the champion, I'm guessing we're probably going to have to beat the champion."

Here we go, Texas.

item.title: Michael J. Fox says going public with Parkinson's was 'hell' on wife Tracy Pollan
item.description: Michael J. Fox reveals the toll going public with his Parkinson's diagnosis has taken on wife Tracy Pollan and why he refused to hide his condition from the world.
item.content:encoded:
Michael J. Fox is opening up about how going public with his battle with Parkinson's has affected not only him, but his loved ones.

As part of the third annual Social Impact Summit, hosted by The Hollywood Reporter and Social Impact Fund ,the 65-year-old actor went into detail about why he chose to go public with his Parkinson's diagnosis, and the toll that decision had, and continues to have on his family, particularly his wife, Tracy Pollan.

"It was hell on Tracy, it was really hard on her — and it still is, because it’s always changing," he told The Hollywood Reporter. "But I said to her, ‘I have to go all the way here. No half measures.’ I started to understand what it was like for these people who didn’t have the options or the choices I had, didn’t have the connections and the reason to be optimistic...I needed to normalize it and just be that all the time. I said, ‘I have to not hide this. F— vanity.’"

The actor was first diagnosed with the degenerative disease in 1991 at the age of 29, keeping his diagnosis a secret for seven years, before going public in 1998 during the third season of his hit show, "Spin City."

VINCE GILL GAVE AMY GRANT A REALITY CHECK DURING RECOVERY FROM BRAIN INJURY

After one more season on the show, Fox chose to retire from acting in order to devote his time to advocacy and fundraising for Parkinson's disease. It was then that he founded the Michael J. Fox Foundation in 2000, which is dedicated towards finding a cure.

Since its inception, the foundation has generated more than $3 billion in research, which has left Fox and Deborah W. Brooks, Co-Founder and CEO of the foundation, optimistic about where the research is headed.

WATCH: Co-founder of The Michael J. Fox Foundation is proud of what they have achieved

"Well, it's been the best state for Parkinson's research ever," Brooks said. "And in large part, so much of that progress has taken place in the course of our organization's existence. Not that we're responsible for everything, but we've scaled so significantly and have been able to activate scientists globally. And that is showing in the results and the pace of progress across our field."

She added: "We have now committed over three billion dollars worth of research worldwide, and so we're working with scientists everywhere, and we work with academics, biotechs and pharma. Our goal is to take aha moments and get them to the drugstore shelf. And so it's been that momentum and particularly a recent breakthrough in how we can detect Parkinson's even before symptoms are the kind of things that tell us. We are onto it, and we are not giving up."

Brooks credits Fox for his "courage and willingness to live his Parkinson's experience in front of the world," calling it "empowering for patients who face similar experiences in reducing stigma."

She also recalled her first board meeting at the foundation, noting it was clear Fox was not one to "just to put his name on something and hope for the best," and that his goal has always been "to do everything it took and get this done and close our doors."

WATCH: Deborah W. Brooks commends Michael J. Fox for his 'courage' to live openly with Parkinson's

"His purity of motive has been core to helping us think how what we're going to prioritize. It shapes our values in terms of speed. Something like trying to cure diseases, that's not a time-neutral agenda item for people who have degenerative and progressive disease. And so I think, you know, this notion that someone puts their voice out or lends their name, it's always been deeper than that for him. And I see that a lot more in philanthropy in general today, but it has been a game changer. For raising awareness, of course, but it's also been a game changer to put those values to work in our mission to cure Parkinson's disease."

Brooks has seen firsthand how Fox's decision to go public with his diagnosis has helped others, saying she gets emotional thinking about his interactions with other patients, adding, "It is so powerful – an experience to be seen and felt when you share the experience of Parkinson's disease."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

While the "Back to the Future" star initially retired from acting in 1998, he returned in his first major on-screen role in 2004 for a guest appearance in "Scrubs," later starring in "The Good Wife," "Boston Legal" and "Curb Your Enthusiasm."

"It’s really prolonged my life and made my life interesting in a way that I didn’t think it would be to this point," Fox told The Hollywood Reporter. "I’m not retired from acting, but if I didn’t act again, it would be for a very good reason. I let Parkinson’s make decisions for me."

WATCH: Deborah W. Brooks gets choked up when thinking of Michael J. Fox interacting with patients

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Most recently, he received an Emmy nomination for his guest role on the hit Apple TV + show, "Shrinking," in which he played a man dealing with Parkinson's who meets Harrison Ford's character, who has also been diagnosed with the disease, in a doctor's office.

"He was happy that I was sharing my experience with him as a character and helping him do what he needed to do," he explained. "He was so loving. I didn’t know what to expect with Harrison because he’s famously a stoic, curmudgeonly guy — and he’s really a sweetheart. He was so affectionate to me and so welcoming to me."

Fox and Brooks received the Philanthropic Organization of the Year award at this year's Social Impact Summit. Other honorees included John Mayer, who received the Philanthropist of the Year Award, and Stephen Curry and Ayesha Curry, who received the Philanthropists of the Year Award.

item.title: Country singer Megan Moroney swears off dating after men 'ruined' her biggest moments
item.description: Megan Moroney says some of her biggest career moments were ruined by the wrong guy, revealing why she's choosing life on tour with no distractions.
item.content:encoded:
Megan Moroney isn't looking for love.

The country music star said some of the biggest moments of her fast-rising career were derailed by the wrong man. Now, Moroney is choosing to take a break from dating, trading romance for life on tour, time with loved ones and, as she put it, "no man, a dog."

"It’s really nice that this is one of the first tours I’ve been on where I’m not dating anyone, so I literally have no distractions," she told Cosmopolitan. "And that sounds really silly, but there have been a lot of big moments and fun times that have been ruined by a guy for me. It’s nice to just have a clear head."

"I’m learning what I like and what I don’t like and what my goals are," she added. "It’s been cool to be so disciplined with my routines, to see what I’m capable of when everything is going like it’s supposed to."

MEGAN MORONEY STUNS IN ORANGE BIKINI DURING FRIEND'S BAHAMAS BACHELORETTE

That doesn't mean she's sworn off dating for good — just for now.

"There is something about having a crush that’s exciting, and when my tour wraps up in October, when I’m back from Europe, I’ll maybe be on the prowl again."

Moroney said "cuffing season" might be calling her name, but life is peaceful right now.

"... It’s been so nice to have my friends and family out [with me] and that not be an aspect of my life that is any sort of problem for me…. Life is peaceful. I got a dog in February. That’s what I needed all along — no man, a dog."

Moroney's decision to hit pause on dating comes after years of public speculation about her love life. The "6 Months Later" singer has been connected to a handful of high-profile country music names, including Morgan Wallen and Riley Green.

The 28-year-old rising star is known for blending witty storytelling with heartbreak-heavy lyrics.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

While fans often try to decode the real-life inspiration behind her lyrics, Moroney said she's become more comfortable embracing the curiosity.

"In ‘Who Hurt You,’ I made it so obvious who it’s about that it was, like, you could google it, and that was the point of ‘Who Hurt You.’ In the past I got frustrated with people," Moroney told Cosmo. "I was trying to hint at things, and then people would be like, ‘Who is it about?’ And I was like, ‘You know what? I’m just gonna lay it out there. I might as well put his first and last name in it.’ Songwriting is fun, and it’s not meant to be taken so seriously."

WATCH: COUNTRY SINGER MEGAN MORONEY SAYS CAREER PREVENTS HER FROM FINDING ‘SOMEONE THAT I WANT TO MARRY RIGHT NOW’

Still, Moroney said she's learned not to apologize for writing from raw emotion — even if listeners sometimes read more into it than she intended.

"It’s confidence that has grown over time, but it’s not being ashamed of how you felt. If you think it’s wrong, then okay. But this is how I felt, so here’s the song about it," Moroney said. "I always try to explain to people that I’ve written songs about these big emotions, so they can feel huge in a song. But I could have felt that way really intensely for 10 minutes and wrote a whole song about it. And so in your head, you’re like, ‘Oh, this must be her whole life.’ And it’s like, ‘Well, actually, I was just heated, and I wrote the song.’ Songs can be written from a few minutes of a feeling. So sometimes it’s not that deep. Other times, it is that deep."

Moroney doesn't plan to shy away from documenting love, heartbreak and everything in between through her music. She said the honesty that fuels her songwriting is ultimately what keeps fans coming back.

"When I get scared of putting out a song because it’s too personal, I think about how it might help somebody, because every time I’ve done it and I’ve taken the risk, people have come up to me, and they’re like, ‘This song made me dump this guy’ and ‘This song is making me never want to go back to him,'" she recalled. "It’s just one of those things where you have to be like, ‘F--k it. What’s the worst that could happen?’"

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Moroney attended the University of Georgia before moving to Nashville to pursue music full-time.

Moroney's breakthrough came in 2022 with the viral hit "Tennessee Orange," a love song that became a country radio staple and launched her into the mainstream. She released her debut album, "Lucky," in 2023.

The singer-songwriter won the ACM award for new female artist of the year and the CMA award for new artist of the year.

She later released her sophomore album, "Am I Okay?," in 2024.

Her newest musical endeavor, "Cloud 9," was released in February. Her tour began in May.

item.title: Teacher who served as GOP state lawmaker allegedly used Roblox to lure student into sexual relationship
item.description: David Pritt allegedly used Roblox and Snapchat to groom a student before engaging in sexual acts at Oak Hill High School, federal charges say.
item.content:encoded:
A West Virginia teacher and Republican state lawmaker is accused of using the popular online gaming platform Roblox to groom a teenage student before allegedly engaging in sexual acts with her at school, according to a federal affidavit obtained by Fox News Digital.

David Elliott Pritt, 36, a Republican member of the West Virginia House of Delegates representing Fayette County, was arrested this week and federally charged with enticement of a minor.

Prosecutors allege Pritt used interstate communications to persuade, induce or entice the girl to engage in illegal sexual activity and produce child sexual abuse material.

TEACHER ACCUSED OF SEX WITH STUDENTS WAS ALLEGEDLY BLACKMAILED FOR BETTER GRADES: WARRANTS

The investigation began after the girl's family reported the allegations to law enforcement on July 9, according to the affidavit obtained and reviewed by Fox News Digital.

The girl told investigators Pritt first contacted her while she was an eighth-grade student during the 2024-25 school year, when he taught middle school in Fayette County. Their conversations allegedly began on Roblox before moving to the social media app Snapchat.

According to the affidavit, the conversations became increasingly personal, and Pritt eventually told the girl he loved her. During the summer of 2025, Pritt allegedly sent the girl a sexually explicit photograph of himself over Snapchat and requested nude photographs from her. The girl told investigators she complied.

FLORIDA MAN WHO ALLEGEDLY LURED WOMEN WITH DATING APP COULD HAVE MORE VICTIMS: AUTHORITIES

After both moved to the local high school, the girl alleged Pritt repeatedly asked her to meet him in his classroom, where she said she performed sexual acts on him approximately five to 10 times. The final encounter allegedly occurred around December 2025 or January 2026.

According to the affidavit, Pritt continued requesting sexually explicit photographs through July 2026 and urged the girl not to tell anyone about their relationship. Investigators said he warned her police would seize their phones and 'open up hell in our lives.' He also allegedly said the allegations 'would be in the news' before adding, 'I would lose my entire life.'

Federal investigators allege there is probable cause to believe Pritt knowingly used interstate communications between May 2025 and July 2026 to entice the minor to produce child sexual abuse material and engage in sexual activity in violation of West Virginia law.

Pritt has represented Fayette County's District 50 in the West Virginia House of Delegates since 2022. He was elected as a Democrat before switching to the Republican Party in 2023.

REPUBLICAN LAWMAKER WELCOMES HOUSE ETHICS PROBE INTO SEXUAL MISCONDUCT ALLEGATIONS AGAINST HIM

State House Speaker Roger Hanshaw called the allegations "shocking, disgusting and disappointing in every way" and removed Pritt from his House roles.

Hanshaw also urged Pritt to resign.

A spokesperson for Hanshaw told Fox News Digital that House leadership has taken the only administrative action currently available by removing Pritt from his House roles and is reviewing the constitutional process for expulsion, which would require the support of two-thirds of elected House members.

"My heart aches for his family, and if any part of these serious allegations are true, the law should be applied in full force," Hanshaw said in a statement.

Fayette County Schools did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's request for comment.

item.title: Carville tells Democrats to 'just quit fighting' against voter ID requirements, warns it polls at 75%
item.description: James Carville warns Democrats that fighting voter ID laws polling at 75% approval is a losing battle and urges the party to concede and move on.
item.content:encoded:
Veteran Democratic strategist James Carville warned Democrats on Thursday that the American public believes they don’t care about voter fraud, and the best thing they can do is stop fighting against voter ID requirements and move on to other issues.

His comments come as Republicans in Congress have been pushing to pass the SAVE Act, which would create a new federal proof of citizenship mandate in the voter registration process and impose requirements for states to keep their rolls clear of ineligible voters, in addition to requiring photo ID to vote in any federal elections.

Carville argued that while he believes there is a negligible amount of voter fraud, refusing to put up photo ID safeguards against it is a terrible hill for Democrats to die on.

"First of all, in Louisiana, you got to show your driver's license to vote," Carville said on "Politics War Room." "I'll have to ask Michael Waldman or Fred Wertheimer, does this really have that much effect? And voter fraud in the United States is an infinitesimal problem. To the extent — I guess you could say it exists, anything could exist. It exists on such a minuscule scale, and it's been proven time and time and time again."

SEN MARK WARNER CALLED OUT IN ABC INTERVIEW FOR INACCURATE VOTER ID CLAIMS

However, he still said the issue cannot be ignored.

"But I don't know if we're not better off saying, ‘Okay, we'll take that alternative idea. Bring your gas bill or your light bill or something,’ because, for whatever reason, this s--- polls at 75%," he said. "It barely exists, and it just doesn't make any sense. Having said that, we are not going to win the war against some kind of ID. It doesn't make any sense. It doesn't fix anything, but maybe you just go along with it and move to the next thing."

His co-host Al Hunt disagreed, however, claiming, "We have voter ID in most states. Most states have voter ID," and that with the Republicans' SAVE Act, they are requiring proof of citizenship, causing hurdles in that many people don’t have passports, many people have difficulty tracking down their birth certificate. And many women will run into difficulties if they changed their surnames when they got married."

"Again, if we have it, just say, ‘Okay, pass the law,’" Carville said of requiring voter ID. "I never had a problem with it. I’m just saying, of all the things — if every poll shows 75% or better say we should have some kind of ID to vote, some say — I don’t know what it is — but s---, just quit fighting, go along with it, and move to the next thing." 

WATCH: ELISSA SLOTKIN SAYS SAVE AMERICA ACT WOULD MAKE IT 'HARD FOR ANY DEMOCRAT' TO WIN AN ELECTION

"Wait a minute," Hunt said. "Let me ask you, what are you going along with? With what?" 

"Whatever the law in Louisiana is. Make it the national law," Carville suggested. Hunt replied that he would probably have no issue with that, but Carville made it clear that he is not in favor of the SAVE Act.

"I'm not talking about the SAVE Act. I'm not talking about the SAVE Act at all. I'm saying just adapt that. But somehow or another, it comes across that we don't want to check the eligibility of a voter," Carville warned.

He argued that voter fraud doesn’t make much sense because it’s risking a hefty punishment for individual votes but suggested that the sooner this is resolved, the better.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

"It's all a stupid argument. But maybe we could just come up with a simple thing and just say, ‘Okay, we're for this. Bye. Thank you.’ Get the hell out of this argument," he said.

"Yeah. Well, you know, I basically agree with that," Hunt replied. "But we really have to deal with the issue better, Democrats really have to deal with the issue better."

item.title: Atlanta Falcons 2026 betting preview: Limited upgrades has new head coach Kevin Stefanski facing a steep climb
item.description: Kevin Stefanski inherits Bijan Robinson, Drake London, and Kyle Pitts but faces a sketchy quarterback situation with Tua Tagovailoa and Michael Penix Jr.
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to our 32-team 2026 NFL Season Preview Series! As we count down to kickoff, we’re breaking down every franchise division-by-division. Today, we’re spotlighting the Atlanta Falcons in the NFC South. Each preview analyzes the team’s offseason moves, coaching staff, projected strengths and weaknesses, schedule, win total and best futures bet.

Atlanta missed the playoffs in 2025-26 for an eighth consecutive season and fired now-former head coach Raheem Morris afterward. The Falcons finished 8-9 and went Under their regular-season win total for a third straight season. This franchise hasn't rebounded since the end of the Matt Ryan Era.

Hiring two-time NFL Coach of the Year Kevin Stefanski to replace Morris is a step in the right direction. But Atlanta made only minimal upgrades to its roster this offseason, ranking 24th in free-agent spending.

DRAKE LONDON SIGNS $141M DEAL WITH FALCONS, BECOMING THIRD HIGHEST-PAID RECEIVER IN NFL: REPORTS

The Falcons finished below .500 last season despite playing the fourth-easiest schedule in the league. This season, Atlanta is projected to play the 20th-easiest schedule. That's just one of the reasons I expect the Falcons to struggle in 2026-27.

For the record, I’m a "Stefanski Guy."

Plus, the NFC South is the softest division in the NFL, and the Falcons have an above-average offensive line, ranked 13th by ESPN’s Mike Clay. They also have a solid core of game-breakers, such as RB Bijan Robinson, WR Drake London and TE Kyle Pitts.

But the NFL is a quarterback-driven league, and Atlanta has a sketchy quarterback situation.

The Falcons took a flyer on the brittle Tua Tagovailoa as an insurance policy for unproven third-year QB Michael Penix Jr., who is just as injury-prone, if not more so, and is coming off a season-ending injury.

Pro Football Focus ranked Penix 35th out of 38 quarterbacks last season and Tagovailoa 32nd. Since Stefanski wasn’t in Atlanta when Penix was drafted and has no allegiance to him, I’m assuming Tua wins the starting job.

Given Stefanski's and Tagovailoa's track records, I wouldn't rule out the Falcons surprising to the good this season. But I wouldn't bet on it.

The Falcons’ defense was their best unit last season. However, defense isn’t as predictive as offense, and Atlanta will be playing a tougher schedule this year.

Stefanski did retain defensive coordinator Jeff Ulbrich, which provides some continuity. Unfortunately, the Falcons lost their leading tackler from last season, LB Kaden Ellis, to the New Orleans Saints in free agency.

FALCONS' JAMES PEARCE JR GRANTED INTERVENTION PROGRAM ENTRY, COULD AVOID TRIAL ON FELONY CHARGES

They could also be without their leading sack-getter, pass-rusher James Pearce Jr., who is being investigated for battery and stalking. Unless those charges are dropped, Pearce will most likely be suspended for the first part of the season.

Whether it’s natural variance or a tougher slate of opposing offenses, Atlanta’s offense will have to do more to support its defense this season.

I’m not counting on that happening.

For argument’s sake, let’s say the Falcons sweep their three home division games. That’s already a friendly projection because I’m expecting improvement from every other NFC South team.

After those three wins, I’m giving Atlanta only one more victory: at the Cleveland Browns in Week 14.

That game carries an obvious revenge angle for Stefanski, who will be coaching against his former employer. Still, it’s no gimme.

The Falcons are a dome team, and it’s going to be brick cold in Cleveland when they face the Browns in the middle of December.

My favorite look for the 2026 Atlanta Falcons is UNDER 5.5 alternate wins (+180) at DraftKings.

Atlanta to finish last in the NFC South at +160 was another option, but as I’ll discuss in a separate article, there is a wide range of possible outcomes for the 2026 New Orleans Saints.

_____________________________

Follow me on X @Geoffery-Clark, and check out my "OutKick Bets Podcast" for more betting content and random rants.

item.title: Six-figure private security bill for top Dem Senate hopeful fuels GOP hypocrisy claims
item.description: Former North Carolina Gov. Roy Cooper spent more than a quarter-million-dollars this election cycle on private security, leading to calls of hypocrisy from critics citing his "soft-on-crime" record.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: Former North Carolina Gov. Roy Cooper’s Senate campaign spent more than $250K on private security, federal records reviewed by Fox News Digital show, giving Republicans a new line of attack as they accuse the Democratic Senate hopeful of backing policies that make North Carolinians feel less safe.

Federal Election Commission records show Cooper’s campaign spent $277,312.28 on security-related payments during the 2026 election cycle thus far, with the money going to two vendors: Foresight Solutions and Frontpoint. Foresight Solutions describes its work as including customized security assessments, workplace safety training, de-escalation techniques, conflict resolution and workplace violence prevention. Frontpoint markets home-security systems designed to protect homes and families.

The spending comes as Cooper faces GOP attacks over his record on policing, criminal justice reform, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) cooperation and COVID-19-era inmate releases during his time as governor. 

Republicans also have invoked the killing of Ukrainian refugee Iryna Zarutska on a Charlotte light rail train, arguing Cooper’s criminal justice posture — including a racial equity task force that backed changes to cash bail — reflects a broader soft-on-crime record. 

SUPREME COURT JUSTICES MAKE RARE CAPITOL HILL APPEARANCE TO PUSH $230M SECURITY BUDGET

Republican strategist Mark Bednar, who was a communications director for former House Speaker Kevin McCarthy and current Transportation Secretary and former Congressman Sean Duffy, ripped Cooper's "blatant hypocrisy" in a statement to Fox News Digital.

"The issue on voters' minds isn't whether Roy Cooper or any other Democrat deems it necessary to keep themselves safe, but the issue is when he and other Democrats prioritize their own security while trying to hide from voters' their soft-on-crime records that threaten local communities," Bednar said.

"As Governor, Roy Cooper agreed to release thousands of criminals early, many of whom went on to commit more crimes, and he fought tooth and nail to prevent local law enforcement working with immigration enforcement officials," he continued. "In an election year that's going to come down to the wire, blatant hypocrisy like this is going to stick out to voters."

However, a Cooper campaign spokesperson pushed back on the criticism, defending the former governor's public-safety record and accusing his Republican opponent in the upcoming general election, Michael Whatley, of hypocrisy.  

WHATLEY LINKS COOPER TO TRANSIT KILLING, ACCUSES DEM OF FREEING ‘RAPISTS AND MURDERERS’

"While Roy spent his career putting rapists and violent criminals behind bars and signing tough on crime, stricter bail and pretrial release laws as governor, Michael Whatley spent his (career) appointing a convicted child sex predator who served time in prison to a powerful position within the North Carolina Republican Party and pushing to release prisoners during the pandemic," the spokesperson said, referring to Whatley's appointment of Harvey Lee West.  

West pleaded guilty in 2000 to five counts of taking indecent liberties with a child and later served on the North Carolina GOP’s Plan of Organization Committee, which helps set party rules, according to PolitiFact. 

The Cooper campaign suggested Republicans have been distorting Cooper’s record on COVID-era inmate releases, pointing to past statements from the North Carolina GOP under Whatley that criticized Cooper for not doing more to address COVID-19 inside prisons.

Cooper’s campaign also pointed to the former governor's 16 years as North Carolina attorney general, where he served as the state’s top law enforcement official, and legislation he signed as governor that made it easier to go after drug dealers, gave judges discretion to keep violent offenders behind bars ahead of trial, and provided raises to law enforcement.

A Whatley spokesperson clapped back at Cooper's claims when asked for a response, arguing the former governor has a long record of mismanaging significant challenges. 

"Roy Cooper has blood on his hands. He chose to release thousands of convicted criminals back onto North Carolina’s streets instead of fighting in court like Democrat governors in Colorado and Illinois," Whatley spokesperson DJ Griffin told Fox News Digital in response to Cooper's attack. "The consequences have been devastating."

item.title: Defense industry leaders align with Trump admin plans in closed-door strategy session on Iran war funding
item.description: Susie Wiles and leaders from Raytheon, Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman and Palantir aligned on $73 billion in Pentagon and intelligence funding.
item.content:encoded:
EXCLUSIVE: High-level Trump administration officials, including Chief of Staff Susie Wiles, met Thursday morning at the White House with key defense industry stakeholders where White House officials laid out their vision for the House of Representatives' current budget reconciliation package related to crucial funding for the Iran war.

Wiles, Department of War and Office of Management and Budget (OMB) leaders, along with leaders from 17 top defense contractors including Raytheon, BAE Systems, Honeywell, Northrop Grumman, Lockheed Martin, Palantir and others, were in attendance for the meeting, according to a Trump administration official. The Trump administration representatives laid out their vision for what is being called "Reconciliation 3.0."

The purpose of the meeting was to ensure that all the companies were aligned on the importance of this reconciliation package being passed, which, according to the administration official, they were.

REPORTER'S NOTEBOOK: HOUSE'S FINAL SPRINT PUTS SAVE AMERICA ACT AND IRAN FUNDING IN PLAY

The budget reconciliation process allows the party in power in Congress to pass sweeping fiscal policy legislation while completely sidelining the other side — in this case, Democrats.

It does so by bypassing the Senate filibuster — which needs 60 votes to overcome — allowing the upper chamber to line up with the House's own simple majority threshold.

Reconciliation 3.0 includes $73 billion for Pentagon and intelligence funding, which is largely tied to replenishing U.S. weapons caches for the Iran war.

GOP'S $95B DEFENSE PACKAGE SURVIVES CRITICAL HURDLE AMID FISCAL HAWK REVOLT

While Wiles and the other Trump administration leaders articulated their vision for the third reconciliation bill, the entire room was bought in and committed to their plan, the source told Fox News Digital.

Further, the defense company leaders spoke favorably about the White House's approach, and said that they were working hard to advocate for lawmakers on the Hill to pass the House budget resolution before the imminent August recess.

The House has passed a framework with cash targets for relevant committees, but now, those committees need to provide details for how that money will be spent. The main players are the House Armed Services Committees and the House Intelligence Committee.

The president himself has vigorously advocated to get the deal done.

"I am asking all House Republicans to VOTE YES this week on the Budget Resolution, which is an important first step towards getting a Budget Bill," he said Monday on TRUTH Social.

item.title: Smithsonian pushed ‘revolting’ social action training after ‘whiteness’ chart apology: expert
item.description: Smithsonian staff co-authored a DEI training toolkit that calls objectivity part of white supremacy, years after the museum apologized for a similar chart.
item.content:encoded:
Smithsonian staff played a pivotal role in developing what one expert referred to as a "revolting" DEI training guide despite the taxpayer-funded institution previously walking back and apologizing for a similar chart on "whiteness," a series of House hearings this week revealed.

Mike Gonzalez, a DEI and identity politics expert at the Heritage Foundation, testified that years after the Smithsonian apologized for its controversial "whiteness" chart, the National Museum of American History continued advancing similar concepts through a training guide known as the "Museums as Sites for Social Action" (MASS) toolkit. Similar to the chart, Gonzalez said the toolkit describes objectivity, individualism, perfectionism and several other concepts as characteristics of white supremacy.

In addition to staff members co-authoring the toolkit, Gonzalez said that for years museum leadership kept "indoctrinating staff" by holding staff reading groups to study its materials.

"[The] museum kept surreptitiously indoctrinating staff with this trash for three years after the museum apologized for using it, which shows the promises of reform are written in sand," he said in one of the hearings this week. "This is their own words, the leadership of the Smithsonian says constantly that it wants to use the museum to transform America, for societal transformation. They see history itself as a tool, an instrument to change society."

WATCH: ‘SHOCKING’ BEN FRANKLIN SMEAR LEAVES SMITHSONIAN CHIEF WITH ‘NO REGRETS’

In 2020, the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture removed and apologized for a chart that described "objective, rational linear thinking" as an aspect of "whiteness" or "white dominant culture," saying, "We erred in including the chart. We have removed it, and we apologize," the Miami Herald reported.

In a viral moment from one of the hearings this week, Rep. Brandon Gill, R-Texas, confronted Smithsonian National Museum of American History Director Anthea Hartig about a passage in the "Museums as Sites for Social Action" (MASS Action) toolkit used by the museum that asserts objectivity is part of white supremacy. In response, Hartig said the museum did not develop the toolkit and asserted "we’re not promoting any ideology."

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

Gonzalez, however, pointed out that the toolkit was co-authored by half a dozen current and former Smithsonian employees, including an NMAH employee, and references Smithsonian materials.

"This is what Marxists do. I think that people who want to truly transform the country, seize the cultural institutions in order to mold how people think," he said.

"But wait, there’s more," said Gonzalez.

'THE VIEW' CO-HOST WHINES SMITHSONIAN HEARINGS ARE ABOUT TRUMP ADMINISTRATION NOT WANTING TO PAY REPARATIONS

He pointed to a 2022 "Excellence in DEAI Report" co-authored by Smithsonian Secretary Lonnie Bunch that praises the MASS Action toolkit as a good resource for the dissemination of formal DEI training.

The report states that "one major aspect of the work that takes time is shifting leaders and museum professionals away from white-dominant characteristics of perfection, risk aversion, and conflict avoidance to move toward action."

Addressing the lawmakers present during the hearing, Gonzalez said, "Does anyone in this committee believe that perfection is a white characteristic? The man who runs the museum apparently does."

WATCH: BRANDON GILL CORNERS LAW SCHOOL GATEKEEPER WITH DEI QUESTION SHE WON'T ANSWER

"Lonnie Bunch himself agrees with this racist nonsense," he charged.

"Once again, I call on Congress to do its duty and take back the Smithsonian from a rogue leadership that now uses it as their own plaything for dangerous ideological games," he continued. "This Insanity has gone on long enough."

Fox News Digital reached out to the Smithsonian Institution and the National Museum of American History for comment.

item.title: French modeling scout linked to Jeffrey Epstein found dead in Paris apartment
item.description: Daniel Siad's name appeared roughly 2,000 times in the Epstein files, and at least 24 women came forward as alleged victims or witnesses in a trafficking probe.
item.content:encoded:
A French modeling scout with ties to Jeffrey Epstein was found dead inside his Paris apartment, prosecutors said.

The body of Daniel Siad, 69, was discovered Monday night, and an autopsy is pending. Siad's attorney, Menya Arab-Tigrine, told Fox News Digital that he died from a heart attack.

"While he died of a heart attack, the unbearable waiting, the stigma cast upon him—and, by extension, his loved ones—as well as the daily pressure and anxiety he endured, undoubtedly played a role," she said. "We must await the autopsy results to determine whether his body simply gave out."

Fox News Digital has reached out to French prosecutors. 

JEFFREY EPSTEIN'S PURPORTED SUICIDE NOTE UNSEALED BY FEDERAL JUDGE IN CELLMATE'S CASE

Siad's name appeared around 2,000 times in the Epstein files released by the Justice Department, Reuters reported.

"I am here in Barcelona I have girls staying with me from Australia Croecian origin, so you can speak with her very Sweet," Siad allegedly wrote to Epstein in one email.

"I am looking to place her here and Paris," he added.

DEMOCRAT CALLS HOWARD LUTNICK A 'PATHOLOGICAL LIAR' AFTER CLOSED-DOOR EPSTEIN TESTIMONY

"Photo?" Epstein replied.

Another email from Siad to Epstein from April 2009 included images of a 17-year-old Slovakian girl while he was scouting models in Bratislava.

At least 24 women have come forward as victims or witnesses of alleged acts committed by Siad or others following the February launch of a human trafficking and fraud probe, Paris prosecutor Laure Beccuau told Reuters.

Investigators looked into Siad, but there wasn't enough evidence to justify his immediate arrest, said Arab-Tigrine.

"Let us not forget that he was never prosecuted, nor was he ever formally implicated in any legal proceedings," she said. "In the coming days, we must respect the grief of his loved ones. He has passed away, and he was innocent."

item.title: Nevada GOP nominee Carrie Buck says she’s ‘coming to retire Dina Titus’
item.description: Carrie Buck sits down at Freedom Fest to discuss her campaign to unseat veteran Democrat incumbent in Las Vegas-based swing district.
item.content:encoded:
After flipping a Democrat-leaning state senate district in 2020, Carrie Buck is setting her sights on a bigger target this election cycle: Nevada's first congressional district, an urban Las-Vegas based district stretching east to the Arizona border.

But it will not be easy to oust Congresswoman Dina Titus, an 8-term veteran, with strong ties to organized labor, the tourism industry, and the business community.

MINIVAN MOM PUTS DEM INCUMBENT ON NOTICE IN TOP GOP TARGET DISTRICT: 'SHE HAS DONE NOTHING FOR US'

Recently, Buck sat down with Fox News Digital at Freedom Fest in Las Vegas, to discuss her swing district appeal, her background as a teacher and principal, and why she believes her take on kitchen table economics will enable her to pull off an upset come election day.

First and foremost, Buck is no stranger to challenging Democrats on their own turf:

"Well, in 2020, I flipped a blue district to Republican, and it represents 20% of this [NV-1] district. So, I know I can flip the entire district. People are just tired of my Democrat opponent, and they're ready for change."

Buck cites her three decades of experience in the Nevada education system as critical to understanding the electorate:

"Well, the past 30 years, I've served in the school system here in Nevada. I was a teacher…I taught adult ESL, I taught children for years. Then I became a principal of one of the lowest performing schools in the state and moved it to a national Title I school. I won the Milken Award in that time. 

"And so I've been breathing life into children and families for years. When I knock on doors, when I'm out in the community, people are like, ‘Dr. Buck, you were my principal.’ And so, I think a lot of people know that I'm going to really deliver for Nevadans in DC."

Housing affordability is a signature issue for Buck.

She pledges to "ensure that Nevadans have access to lower-cost housing" while also "driving down interest rates so that people can buy whatever house that they want to."

TOP GOP TARGET DINA TITUS FENDS OFF HOUSE PRIMARY CHALLENGERS

Buck also argues that the immigration crisis during the Biden administration was a key driver on the housing affordability issue:

"I think also what impacted housing is the immigration policies of the Biden years, where they allowed all these people into our country and that drove up [the cost of] all of our low-income housing. 

"It depleted it. And so now that we're seeing people end up getting deported and going back to their original country, I know that it's definitely driving down prices here."

Buck promises that taking a hard line on the national debt will be a top priority:

"Spending is definitely out of control when we look at the waste of taxpayer dollars, waste, fraud and abuse. When we've seen some of these cases from across the nation, it really is a red alert. We need to make sure that we root that out. 

"I just actually saw the vice president is heading up this task force, and they're finding lots of fraud…We can't just release taxpayer dollars. So reining in the waste, fraud and abuse and making sure that [government spending] goes to the seniors and the children and the veterans who need it."

Buck is a strong supporter of pathways to citizenship for immigrants, but also maintains a hard line on issues of border security and illegal immigration:

"There is a pathway to citizenship, and I think we need to get people signed up. I've been part of a movement to ensure that people have access to get citizenship. And that's the way, the legal way.

"But coming here illegally, you are breaking laws. Those people need to be deported. We've seen so many victims of crimes by illegal immigrants, and that has to stop. We need to make sure that we have safe communities. I'm a wife of a law enforcement officer and know how important our safe communities and our men and women are working out there in law enforcement."

Water issues remain an enormous concern west of the Mississippi, and particularly in Nevada. Buck argues that California could and should play a large role in ensuring Nevadans access to water:

"For too long, Nevada has been the conservationist…Our citizens conserve so much water…All it would take is California to build a basin and hold their rainwater and not release it into the Pacific. 

"But they're very selfish neighbors…We need to make sure that our federal government can maybe step in and build these reservoirs to hold water."

item.title: Read the list: 44 lawmakers swept into Jack Smith text sweep as his legal reckoning grows
item.description: Jack Smith's probe secretly seized text messages from 44 lawmakers, including Jim Jordan and Chuck Grassley, tied to January 6 and classified documents.
item.content:encoded:
Former special counsel Jack Smith’s team obtained and reviewed the contents of text messages exchanged between Trump White House officials and 44 current and former members of Congress, according to Justice Department records released by two Republican-led Senate committees.

The messages were contained in White House records subpoenaed from the National Archives covering Oct. 1, 2020, through Jan. 20, 2021. The Justice Department said Smith’s investigative team "apparently bypassed" a filter team established to screen potentially privileged material before investigators accessed it.

House Judiciary Committee Chairman Jim Jordan, R-Ohio, on Wednesday issued a criminal referral for Smith, arguing that he misled Congress when he stated that he did not obtain or review lawmakers' text messages while carrying out his probes into President Donald Trump. The referral functions as an invitation for the Justice Department to launch an investigation into Smith's conduct.

JACK SMITH THRUST BACK INTO SPOTLIGHT AT BLANCHE HEARING OVER EXPOSED SENATORS’ MESSAGES

Read the list of 44 lawmakers:

Among the senators targeted in the probe were: Sens. Chuck Grassley, R-Iowa; Ron Johnson, R-Wis.; John Cornyn, R-Texas; Lindsey Graham, R-S.C.; Josh Hawley, R-Mo.; Mike Lee, R-Utah; Cory Booker, D-N.J.; Cindy Hyde-Smith, R-Miss.; Dan Sullivan, R-Alaska; David Perdue, R-Ga.; Lamar Alexander, R-Tenn.; Martha McSally, R-Ariz.; Rand Paul, R-Ky.; Rick Scott, R-Fla.; Rob Portman, R-Ohio; Roy Blunt, R-Mo.; Susan Collins, R-Maine; Tim Scott, R-S.C.; Tom Cotton, R-Ark.; and Cory Gardner, R-Colo.

On the House side, the investigation targeted: Reps. Kevin McCarthy, R-Calif.; Steve Scalise, R-La.; Jim Jordan, R-Ohio; Devin Nunes, R-Calif.; Adam Smith, D-Wash.; Andy Biggs, R-Ariz.; Mark Walker, R-N.C.; Bryan Steil, R-Wis.; Dan Newhouse, R-Wash.; Doug Collins, R-Ga.; Dusty Johnson, R-S.D.; Elise Stefanik, R-N.Y.; John Katko, R-N.Y.; Josh Gottheimer, D-N.J.; Karen Bass, D-Calif.; Ken Buck, R-Colo.; Lee Zeldin, R-N.Y.; Mario Diaz-Balart, R-Fla.; Mo Brooks, R-Ala.; Morgan Griffith, R-Va.; Rodney Davis, R-Ill.; Russ Fulcher, R-Idaho; Scott Perry, R-Pa.; and Thomas Massie, R-Ky.

Fox News Digital reached out to every current lawmaker named on the list.

Grassley and Johnson, through their senior committee positions, were responsible for the release of the new documents.

Several lawmakers on the list had publicly documented roles in efforts surrounding the 2020 election, including those who have since condemned Smith’s review of their communications.

Sen. Chuck Grassley

As president pro tempore of the Senate, Grassley played a procedural role on Jan. 6, 2021, as Congress moved to certify the results of the 2020 presidential election. The senator, for instance, called the Senate into session before senators moved to the House's chamber to participate in a joint session where the results of each state would be certified.

Prior to Jan. 6, Grassley suggested that he may preside over the Senate’s debate surrounding disputes over the 2020 election if Vice President Mike Pence didn’t show up.

"Jack Smith’s criminal investigation of President Trump was a runaway train that had no brakes," Grassley said of his messages being collected. "Based on the information that’s been produced to me and Senator Johnson, Biden DOJ and FBI investigators apparently ignored their own routine investigative protocols to obtain and review work-related messages from me and dozens of my Republican and Democrat colleagues who were outside the scope of the government’s investigation."

Sen. Ron Johnson

In the lead-up to Jan. 6, Johnson joined a group of Republican senators calling for an emergency 10-day audit of disputed election results and saying they would vote against certifying electors from contested states unless such a review occurred.

Documents released by the House Jan. 6 committee in early 2023 reported that Johnson was communicating with former chairman of the Wisconsin Republican Party about a plan to have the state’s GOP-controlled legislature choose its presidential electors. The senator responded that he had no recollection of the conversation and accused the committee of trying to smear him.

Republicans were intensely critical of the House Jan. 6 committee, arguing that it lacked a meaningful voice for the opposition seeing as then-House Speaker Nancy Pelosi, D-Calif., selected every member while rejecting the nominations put forward by McCarthy. They also argued that the committee focused too little on security and intelligence failures ahead of the breach of the Capitol.

"This is yet another grotesque example of the Biden administration’s weaponization of the Justice Department," Johnson said of the seized text messages. "Jack Smith’s team acted with impunity as they disregarded their own protocols to obtain and access White House text messages, including messages to and from 44 Members of Congress. This is precisely why Chairman Grassley and I have worked to uncover the truth, bring transparency to the American people, and hold accountable those who weaponized federal law enforcement."

BIDEN SPECIAL COUNSEL’S 'RUNAWAY TRAIN’ SCOOPED UP SENSITIVE LAWMAKER INFO: 'ABUSE OF POWER'

Sen. Lindsey Graham

In the days after the 2020 election, Graham urged Trump not to concede. He later donated to Trump’s legal defense fund and, as chairman of the Senate Judiciary Committee, opened inquiries into election procedures in several battleground states and contacted state election officials.

The late South Carolina senator grew frustrated with Trump and his followers as their objections continued to fail in court and fully broke with the movement following the riot on Jan. 6.

"Count me out," he said at the time. "Enough is enough."

Sen. Josh Hawley

Hawley is notable in that he, on Dec. 30, 2020, became the first senator to announce his intention to formally object to Electoral College votes during the certification process. His decision was consequential as federal law required objections from both a representative and a senator to force a debate on the election.

WATCH: HAWLEY FUMES AFTER 4 GOP SENATORS HELP SINK TRUMP-BACKED VOTER ID LAW

Trump attempted to call Hawley six times ahead of the Jan. 6 breach, according to White House call logs obtained by the House Jan. 6 select committee, showing that there was a line of communication between the two.

Though some senators abandoned their objections to the 2020 election after the Capitol was breached, Hawley maintained his objections and voted against certifying the results from Arizona and Pennsylvania.

Hawley called the documents released by Grassley and Johnson "more evidence of Biden corruption" adding that, "Jack Smith illegally scooped up the text messages of Senators — including mine."

Sen. Mike Lee

Text messages turned over by former White House chief of staff Mark Meadows to the House Jan. 6 committee show that Lee discussed possible legal and constitutional avenues for challenging the 2020 election results.

Lee initially told Meadows that he supported exhausting every available legal and constitutional remedy to address concerns about the election. By Jan. 3, 2021, however, he warned that the effort "could all backfire badly," according to text messages Meadows turned over to the House Jan. 6 committee.

The senator ultimately voted against sustaining objections to Arizona’s and Pennsylvania's election results.

Mike Lee slammed Smith's collection of text messages this month as "un-American" and argued that it came "uncomfortably close to spying on a coequal, coordinate branch."

Rep. Steve Scalise

Scalise, as House Republican whip, was responsible for convincing representatives to vote in line with the party’s interests, providing a straightforward reason as to why White House staff may have been in contact with him.

Further, a publicly released deposition shows that a Scalise aide communicated with Pence’s legislative-affairs director regarding a Jan. 3 call organized by Jordan involving Trump, Meadows and members pressing certification objections.

SCALISE URGES PRITZKER TO 'LOOK AT THE MAN IN THE MIRROR' AFTER HE BLAMES TRUMP FOR POLITICAL VIOLENCE

The Louisiana congressman called congressional action the "only remaining recourse" for addressing what he described as improper changes to election laws in several states.

While he condemned rioting, Scalise, like Hawley, ultimately voted to reject Biden’s electoral votes from both Arizona and Pennsylvania.

Rep. Jim Jordan

Jordan participated in efforts to challenge the 2020 election results, including discussions about delaying congressional certification and objecting to electoral votes.

On Jan. 2, the Ohio congressman led a conference call with Trump and other members of Congress to discuss strategies for delaying the Jan. 6 joint session, according to the House Jan. 6 committee’s final report.

Jordan also reportedly had a 10-minute-long call with Trump on the morning of Jan. 6, according to publicly released phone records. After the breach of the Capitol, Jordan condemned the violence but voted to sustain objections to the electoral results in Arizona and Pennsylvania.

"You can’t do that. As Senator Hawley just said, this is a separation of powers issue," Jordan told Fox News host Sean Hannity earlier this month. "There’s a speech or debate clause. It was supposed to be filtered and screened. It wasn’t. It was just given from the National Archives to Jack Smith’s team."

Smith’s attorneys called the criminal referral "meritless" Thursday in a letter previously obtained by Fox News Digital.

"The House Judiciary Committee’s referral to the Department of Justice of our client Jack Smith is based on a spurious claim that Mr. Smith provided false testimony during his eight-hour deposition on December 17, 2025. The referral is made not because Mr. Smith made a false statement—the letter concedes he did not—but because he did not proactively disclose information that was not responsive to a question asked," the lawyer wrote.

"During his testimony, Mr. Smith provided truthful, responsive answers and candidly discussed his work as Special Counsel. This referral reflects a chilling eagerness to weaponize the justice system by attacking a career, nonpartisan public servant who faithfully and fearlessly performed his duties by following the facts and the law."

Fox News Digital's Elizabeth Elkind contributed to this report.

item.title: Trump can't help but take jab at Mets in speech while praising Braves CEO: 'They lose all the time'
item.description: President Donald Trump told a Georgia crowd the New York Mets "lose all the time" despite their top payroll, praising Atlanta Braves CEO Mike Plant as someone they should "steal."
item.content:encoded:
The New York Mets are not safe anywhere.

Two years after making the National League Championship Series, the Mets are on pace to lose 95 games, which would be their most since also losing 95 in 2003. But this season is much more embarrassing than that.

The Mets came into this season overhauling a roster that choked away the best record in the league last summer, which turned in a league—high $329 million payroll.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

But it's been all for naught, and the president of the United States has taken notice.

"The Mets have the highest salary in baseball, and they lose all the time," President Donald Trump said to a crowd in Georgia - probably filled with happy Atlanta Braves fans.

The Los Angeles Dodgers, in fact, have the most money committed to their roster, but in terms of the 2026 payrolls, the Mets are tops in the league thanks to the Dodgers' deferrals.

Trump made the comment in praising Braves Development Company CEO Mike Plant, saying the Mets should "maybe steal this guy."

"You don't realize how good he is. There are teams where they never go down. Then you have other teams, they've been rebuilding for years. I don't want to mention teams, because I know all the owners are old friends of mine, I get myself in trouble, but they're always rebuilding. This guy never rebuilds. He just stays at the top. He's amazing," Trump said. "And then he loses the best player. He loses a great first baseman (Freddie Freeman), I would say great first baseman, and they keep going along and they get things for the first baseman. And he was really good, Dodgers got him. But I watched the Braves for years and they've done such a great job."

YANKEES PLAYER'S PITCH CLOCK ANTICS PROMPT WILD HOT-MIC MOMENT, BACKLASH: 'YOU'RE PICKING ON ME'

The Mets payroll is headlined by Juan Soto's record-breaking $765 million contract over 15 years that he signed in December 2024. Francisco Lindor is also making nearly $34 million.

The Mets let Pete Alonso and Edwin Diaz walk in free agency while trading Jeff McNeil and Brandon Nimmo, and brought in Jorge Polanco, Luis Robert, Bo Bichette, Devin Williams and Luke Weaver. However, despite the Mets ranking 27th in batting average, 26th in runs scored, and 28th in OPS, while their rotation has the ninth-worst ERA in the big leagues.

Polanco is hitting .165 in an injury-riddled season, and Robert is hitting .211 after injuries. Bichette is on pace for the worst season of his career, Semien is hitting .209 with a .606 OPS, and Williams owns a 4.54 ERA (Weaver's 1.98 ERA is a lone bright spot).

New York is said to have just about everyone available in the trade market, except Soto and some young talent on the big league roster.

The Mets are 43-60 and are 17.0 games back of the Braves in the NL East.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: Co-pilot becomes sick midair as crew illness reports emerge after overnight layover
item.description: An investigation is underway after a British Airways pilot became ill on Flight BA276 from Hyderabad to London. Crew members reportedly got sick during an overnight layover in India.
item.content:encoded:
At least one British Airways pilot became ill during a flight from India to London after multiple crew members reportedly fell sick following an overnight layover in Hyderabad, India.

The incident took place earlier this week, on British Airways Flight BA276 from Hyderabad Rajiv Gandhi International Airport (HYD) to London Heathrow (LHR).

British Airways confirmed to Fox News Digital that one of its pilots became unwell during the flight, but said the aircraft continued safely to its destination and that the safety of the flight was never compromised.

WHY A BLOWN-OUT PLANE WINDOW NEARLY SUCKED A PASSENGER OUTSIDE AT 16,000 FEET

"The welfare of our colleagues is a top priority, and we are reviewing crew accommodation in this location following some isolated reports of sickness," the airline said.

The crew had traveled to Hyderabad on an earlier flight; they had stayed overnight there as part of their normal long-haul layover.

The pilots ate breakfast together in the hotel lounge, according to The Sun — and all of them drank bottled water provided by an outside supplier.

The outlet reported that on the way back to London, the flight's co-pilot was "incapacitated" and had to be put on oxygen.

DELTA FLIGHT FORCED TO DIVERT AFTER PASSENGER GOES INTO LABOR AND DELIVERS BABY MIDAIR

The outlet also reported that two remaining pilots continued operating the aircraft but reportedly became more ill after landing in the U.K.

British Airways did not confirm those reported details when contacted by Fox News Digital.

The flight lasted about 10 hours and 30 minutes, according to FlightAware.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS

The incident sparked discussion online, with some users on a Reddi thread questioning how multiple pilots could become ill at the same time.

"I thought the rule was that pilots/captains cannot eat the same meal during a flight, in case one was dodgy," one person speculated. "But it seems this was the hotel’s breakfast food [that] got them."

"It could also be the water that’s contaminated," another observed.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

Others couldn't resist drawing comparisons to the 1980 comedy "Airplane!" — which centers on a flight whose crew is incapacitated by food poisoning.

"Wasn't there a movie made about this?" one person asked.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"Yes, the great documentary ‘Airplane!'" a Redditor joked.

A third person wrote, "Surely you can't be serious."

Another replied with the film's famous line: "I am [serious], and don't call me Shirley."

item.title: Trump endorsement fails to clear GOP field as former governor joins South Carolina Senate race
item.description: Mark Sanford joins a crowded GOP field challenging Trump-endorsed Darline Graham in South Carolina's special Senate primary set for Aug. 11.
item.content:encoded:
Former congressman and former South Carolina Gov. Mark Sanford, a GOP critic of President Donald Trump, launched a Senate bid on Thursday as he became the latest high-profile Republican running for the seat of the late Sen. Lindsey Graham and joins a crowded field against Trump's endorsed candidate.

"We’re at a tipping point, and that’s why I’ve decided to run for the U.S. Senate," Sanford said in a launch video. "I’d ask that you look at my record in Congress and as governor in watching out for your family’s future."

Sen. Darline Graham, who was appointed last week to fill the remainder of her late brother's term, announced Monday in an interview on Fox News' "Hannity" that she's running for a full six-year term in the Senate. Graham's announcement came three days after she met with Trump at the White House, and the president gave her his "complete and total endorsement" as he encouraged her to run.

But Trump's backing of the late senator's sister isn't stopping other well-known South Carolina Republicans from jumping into the contest.

WHO'S UP AND WHO'S DOWN: CHECK OUT THE FOX NEWS POWER RANKINGS ON THE MIDTERM ELECTIONS

Republican Rep. Ralph Norman, who unsuccessfully campaigned this year for the GOP gubernatorial nomination in the race to succeed term-limited South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster, announced his intention to run this past weekend.

Two-term GOP Rep. Russell Fry followed on Monday. And businessman Mark Lynch, who lost to Lindsey Graham by nearly 30 points in last month's GOP Senate primary, also announced he'd take another shot at the Republican nomination.

The special Republican Senate primary will be held on Aug. 11. If no candidate tops 50%, a runoff election will be held among the top two voter-getters on Aug. 25.

WHAT DARLINE GRAHAM TOLD FOX NEWS' SEAN HANNITY

Sanford, who served three terms in the U.S. House in the 1990s, won election as South Carolina governor in 2002 and was re-elected four years later. A rising conservative star in the GOP, he grabbed national attention and derailed his political career following an extramarital affair in 2009. But Sanford made a political comeback, winning election to his old seat in Congress in 2012 and served three terms.

He briefly launched a longshot and unsuccessful presidential nomination challenge against Trump in the 2020 primaries.

Known as a fiscally conservative budget hawk, Sanford pointed to the nation's debt as a key motivator behind his decision to launch a Senate campaign.

"Our country is at a financial tipping point, and I can’t sit on the sidelines while Washington adds a trillion dollars to the debt every 100 days," Sanford explained. "I’m worried for our children because for the first time, America is borrowing from the future faster than we’re building it."

Unlike Sanford, Fry and Norman, who are both Trump allies in the House, are highlighting their support for the president.

"South Carolina doesn't need just another senator. It needs President Trump's strongest ally in the United States Senate. Make no mistake: I will be President Trump’s favorite U.S. Senator, and you will have a MAGA fighter you can count on," Fry wrote in a social media post as he announced his Senate run.

DEMOCRACY ’26: STAY UP TO DATE WITH THE FOX NEWS ELECTION HUB

Fry described himself as a "MAGA warrior" in an accompanying video, which included pictures of him smiling alongside Trump.

Norman emphasized that he's "running to represent the people of South Carolina in the U.S. Senate because we need a fighter who will stand with President Trump and carry on Lindsey Graham’s legacy!"

And pointing to Trump's top legislative priority, he noted, "I want to get the SAVE America Act passed in the Senate!"

Republican Sens. Mike Lee of Utah and Rick Scott of Florida, conservatives and staunch Trump allies in the chamber, both cited Norman's support for Trump's election reform bill as they went against the president and endorsed the congressman. Norman is also being backed by the political arm of the powerful conservative group Turning Point USA.

Norman also landed the backing of Rep. Nancy Mace of South Carolina. The endorsement by Mace, first reported by Fox News Digital, came after the retiring congresswoman who earlier this year unsuccessfully ran for governor, announced on Monday that she wouldn't run for the Senate.

Besides the president's powerful endorsement, Graham does have the backing of heavy hitters in Congress, including Senate Majority Whip John Barrasso, R-Wyo., who said, "Darline Graham is an excellent senator. She’ll continue to be an excellent senator, and she has my full support."

Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., however, is staying out of the race for now.

Thune said that Graham is a "strong candidate," and though she lacked elected experience, he noted she was "very accomplished in her own life."

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

"She has a right, like everybody else, to get in that race," Thune said.

Backed by Trump's endorsement and enjoying plenty of name recognition thanks to her well-known last name, pundits view Darline Graham as a frontrunner in the three-week sprint to the Republican primary. And the winner of the GOP nomination will be considered the clear favorite against Democratic nominee Annie Andrews in the general election in the solidly red southeastern state.

Trump's endorsements aren't infallible, but his track record so far this cycle, albeit a couple of setbacks, is impressive.

Veteran Republican strategist and communicator Ryan Williams noted that "the Trump endorsement is a major boost for any candidacy, but it doesn’t necessarily give you a lock on the seat."

"Senate seats in South Carolina do not come up very often, and politics is all about timing. You’ve got to seize the opportunity when it presents itself," Williams told Fox News Digital. "If you’re an aspiring House member in South Carolina, this is your time to run, regardless of whom the president is supporting."

And Williams, referring to Norman and Fry, noted, "There’s no downside for running. They’re going to run as Trump Republicans, and they’re going to flatter the president on the campaign trail."

That's likely not the case for Sanford, who didn't mention Trump in his social media post or his accompanying campaign launch video.

Fox News' Liz Elkind contributed to this story.

item.title: Jen Psaki worries Democrats are being drowned out by Trump press coverage, suggests 'maybe we're all at fault'
item.description: MS NOW host Jen Psaki questioned whether she and others in the media have contributed to the Democrats' struggle to break through President Donald Trump's daily dominance of the news.
item.content:encoded:
MS NOW host Jen Psaki worried on Wednesday that the Democratic Party was having a hard time breaking through in the current media environment, wondering if she was partly to blame because President Donald Trump often dominates the news cycle, even on her own show.

Psaki spoke to former ABC News journalist Terry Moran, who said Democrats didn't have the lead one would expect ahead of November's midterm elections, as Trump's approval ratings remain low.

Psaki said enthusiasm was high for Democrats, but that the party had a hard time presenting themselves as something more than anti-Trump.

"It has taken some time for a lot of the leading Democratic voices to move from just anti-Trump to more than anti-Trump, right? To, ‘We’re not for Trump, but here’s what we’re offering,’" she said.

JOHN FETTERMAN RIPS DEMOCRATS FOR RUNNING CAMPAIGNS ON 'F--- TRUMP' INSTEAD OF CONSTRUCTIVE MESSAGING

"I would also say, to take a little bit of the blame off of leading Democrats, it’s very hard to break through with that stuff," Psaki added. "And I realize I have a platform, you have a platform — maybe we’re all at fault for this — but because the things Trump is doing every day are so crazy, it is hard for Democrats to break through with, ‘Here’s my plan for covering children under the age of six with additional health care benefits. Here’s my plan for giving more assistance to pre-K.'"

The MS NOW host said it was hard to decide whom she was going to book outside the Trump-focused news reaction, and said, "Maybe that's a mistake."

"So, I think there’s a lot of factors at play here, but a big one is I don’t think the public knows enough about the Democrats’ plans for making their lives better, as much as they don’t like Trump," she said.

TUNING OUT: WHY MANY AMERICANS ARE SICK OF THE NEWS – ESPECIALLY TRUMP NEWS

Moran agreed with Psaki, and questioned how journalists should approach the issue.

"And, you know, he is chaos and dominates every second of every day as much as he can," he said. "And so that does leave any journalist, any program, any independent journalist, broadcast journalist, with the same problem: Do you cover the Trump show, or do you cover the Trump administration and what people should know about it?"

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

In April, Psaki told Stephen A. Smith she didn't know why some people on the left were spending so much time talking about invoking the 25th Amendment against President Donald Trump.

"I think there are times, I think a lot of people on — and I can't speak for everybody on the left, I don't agree with everything everybody says on the left either — you know, sometimes it's like, not constructive," Psaki said at the time. 

"I mean, you've talked about the 25th Amendment," she continued. "I have no issue with people saying they're for invoking the 25th Amendment, but it's not going to happen. So, it's like why are we spending so much time, you know?"

item.title: Trump's patience 'running out' as Thune fires back over stalled election security bill
item.description: Karoline Leavitt says Trump's patience is running thin with John Thune over the SAVE America Act as Senate Republicans face a 50-vote math problem.
item.content:encoded:
The fight over President Donald Trump’s SAVE America Act has moved from squabbles within the GOP to the top Senate Republican and White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt publicly sniping at one another.

Leavitt suggested on Thursday that Trump’s patience was wearing thin with Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., over his flagship election security legislation.

"His patience is running out. He wants to see as much of the Save America [Act] passed as possible by the August recess," Leavitt said of the heavy-handed president. "He knows that's what the American people want to see passed."

REPUBLICAN SAYS TRUMP'S TOP ELECTION PRIORITY 'DEAD' IN SENATE AS GOP FRACTURES AHEAD OF MIDTERMS

"And I would just add, you know, the American people … elected overwhelmingly Republicans to lead not just this White House, but also the House and the Senate," she continued. "The House did its job. The Senate needs to do its job, too."

But in the Senate, the political reality looks much different than Leavitt’s assertion that Republicans in the upper chamber weren’t doing enough. Still, Thune has become the dartboard for conservatives, online and at 1600 Pennsylvania Ave., to throw their frustrations over the SAVE America Act at.

The SAVE America Act is universally despised by Senate Democrats, who argue that it would disenfranchise millions of Americans from voting. Thune would need Democrats to pass the bill, compared to the House, where Republicans can ram through legislation without Democratic help.

TRUMP'S VOTER ID BILL CATCHES UNLIKELY BREAK AS MCCONNELL REMAINS SIDELINED

Further compounding the issue is the fact that not every Republican is on board with the legislation.

And even though House Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., and Trump are hoping that Thune and Republicans can pass a version of the SAVE America Act through the party-line budget reconciliation process, which requires 50 votes to work, there’s no guarantee that the support is there.

"Well, maybe she or somebody else ought to get on the phone and get the votes, right? It's 50 around here," Thune said. "So instead of, you know, pointing the finger at Republicans, they might think about going after the people who are stopping it on the floor, which is the Democrats. And if there are Republicans that they think are gettable, get on the phone. Let's get them to yes."

GOP CIVIL WAR ERUPTS OVER TRUMP'S LATEST AGENDA PUSH AS KEY REPUBLICANS THREATEN TO SINK MEGABILL

And in the Senate, Republicans are growing increasingly frustrated with the expectations that are being made surrounding the bill that aren’t feasible given the math problem bogging the legislation down in the upper chamber.

Thune is considering using the reconciliation process to keep the government open, too, in the event that Democrats renege in ongoing negotiations to craft a short-term funding deal as the typical government funding process struggles to move forward.

Sen. Thom Tillis, R-N.C., one of the most vocal opponents of the SAVE America Act, said that Republicans should turn to reconciliation to fund the government, and that some were "being dishonest to the American people to say that we need SAVE Act passed because there's no way on God's green Earth it can be implemented by the election."

When asked about Leavitt’s comments against Thune, Tillis dug in.

"I have 1,000% confidence in Thune, and anybody in the White House who thinks he's not a good leader isn't qualified for their job," he said.

item.title: Iran war could pave the way for a former fan-favorite track to rejoin the Formula 1 calendar
item.description: The Middle East conflict has forced F1 to cancel races in Bahrain and Saudi Arabia, and now Malaysia's Sepang Circuit could make a shock return.
item.content:encoded:
The situation in the Middle East has already cost Formula 1 a pair of races in the early part of the season, but it could also lead to a former fan-favorite track making a shock return.

A new report from ESPN says that Malaysia's Sepang Circuit is in the running to make a return if the series can't schedule a make-up date for the Bahrain Grand Prix.

The Bahrain Grand Prix and Saudi Arabian Grand Prix were both scheduled for April, but the ongoing conflict in the Middle East forced F1 to take both off the calendar, causing a month-long gap in the schedule.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE OUTKICK SPORTS COVERAGE

While the race in Saudi Arabia on the Jeddah Corniche Street Circuit has been sidelined entirely this season, the hope was that Bahrain could potentially slot into the weekend of Oct. 4.
